chunk_summary: Familial hypercholesterolemia in children requires early recognition, cascade screening, and statin therapy based on LDL-C levels and risk factors to reduce lifetime ASCVD risk.
learning_objectives: Understand diagnostic criteria for familial hypercholesterolemia in children, including LDL-C thresholds and clinical signs.
Learn the process of cascade screening for familial hypercholesterolemia in first-degree relatives.
Identify appropriate statin therapy initiation based on LDL-C levels and risk factors in pediatric patients.
key_facts: Familial hypercholesterolemia should be suspected with LDL-C ≥160 mg/dL and family history of premature coronary disease or LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL without family history.
Secondary causes of hypercholesterolemia must be excluded before confirming familial hypercholesterolemia.
Cascade screening of parents and first-degree relatives is essential for early detection.
Statin therapy is recommended from age ≥10 years if LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL after 3–6 months of lifestyle therapy or LDL-C 160–189 mg/dL with high-risk factors.
Atorvastatin pediatric dosing typically starts at 10 mg daily, with a range of 10–20 mg/day.
reference_quote: The NHLBI framework supports considering statin therapy from age ≥10 years when LDL-C remains ≥190 mg/dL after 3–6 months of lifestyle therapy, or when LDL-C is 160–189 mg/dL with high-level risk factors.
document_key_topics: Statin therapy guidelines for pediatric dyslipidemia
